lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Nor heaven nor earth have been at peace to-night:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Thrice hath Calpurnia in her sleep cried out,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: “Help, ho! they murder Caesar!” Who’s within?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Go bid the priests do present sacrifice

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And bring me their opinions of success.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: I will, my lord.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: What mean you, Caesar, think you to walk forth?

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: You shall not stir out of your house to-day.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Caesar shall forth: the things that threaten’d me

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Ne’er look’d but on my back; when they shall see

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: The face of Caesar, they are vanished.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Caesar, I never stood on ceremonies,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Yet now they fright me. There is one within,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Besides the things that we have heard and seen,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Recounts most horrid sights seen by the watch.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: A lioness hath whelped in the streets;

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: And graves have yawn’d, and yielded up their dead;

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Fierce fiery warriors fought upon the clouds,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: In ranks and squadrons and right form of war,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Which drizzled blood upon the Capitol;

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: The noise of battle hurtled in the air,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Horses did neigh, and dying men did groan,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: And ghosts did shriek and squeal about the streets.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: O Caesar! these things are beyond all use,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: And I do fear them.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What can be avoided

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Whose end is purposed by the mighty gods?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Yet Caesar shall go forth; for these predictions

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Are to the world in general as to Caesar.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: When beggars die, there are no comets seen;

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: The heavens themselves blaze forth the death of princes.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Cowards die many times before their deaths;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: The valiant never taste of death but once.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Of all the wonders that I yet have heard,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: It seems to me most strange that men should fear;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Seeing that death, a necessary end,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Will come when it will come.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What say the augurers?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: They would not have you to stir forth to-day.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Plucking the entrails of an offering forth,

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: They could not find a heart within the beast.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: The gods do this in shame of cowardice:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Caesar should be a beast without a heart,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: If he should stay at home to-day for fear.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: No, Caesar shall not: danger knows full well

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: That Caesar is more dangerous than he:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: We are two lions litter’d in one day,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And I the elder and more terrible:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And Caesar shall go forth.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Alas, my lord,

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Your wisdom is consumed in confidence.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Do not go forth to-day: call it my fear

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: That keeps you in the house, and not your own.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: We’ll send Mark Antony to the senate-house;

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: And he shall say you are not well to-day:

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Let me, upon my knee, prevail in this.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Mark Antony shall say I am not well;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And, for thy humour, I will stay at home.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Here’s Decius Brutus, he shall tell them so.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Caesar, all hail! good morrow, worthy Caesar:

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: I come to fetch you to the senate-house.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And you are come in very happy time,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: To bear my greeting to the senators

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And tell them that I will not come to-day:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Cannot, is false, and that I dare not, falser:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I will not come to-day: tell them so, Decius.

lines.speaker: CALPURNIA
lines.text: Say he is sick.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Shall Caesar send a lie?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Have I in conquest stretch’d mine arm so far,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: To be afraid to tell graybeards the truth?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Decius, go tell them Caesar will not come.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Most mighty Caesar, let me know some cause,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Lest I be laugh’d at when I tell them so.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: The cause is in my will: I will not come;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: That is enough to satisfy the senate.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: But for your private satisfaction,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Because I love you, I will let you know:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Calpurnia here, my wife, stays me at home:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: She dreamt to-night she saw my statua,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Which, like a fountain with an hundred spouts,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Did run pure blood; and many lusty Romans

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Came smiling, and did bathe their hands in it:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And these does she apply for warnings, and portents,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And evils imminent; and on her knee

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Hath begg’d that I will stay at home to-day.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: This dream is all amiss interpreted;

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: It was a vision fair and fortunate:

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Your statue spouting blood in many pipes,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: In which so many smiling Romans bathed,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Signifies that from you great Rome shall suck

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Reviving blood, and that great men shall press

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: For tinctures, stains, relics and cognizance.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: This by Calpurnia’s dream is signified.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And this way have you well expounded it.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: I have, when you have heard what I can say:

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: And know it now: the senate have concluded

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: To give this day a crown to mighty Caesar.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: If you shall send them word you will not come,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Their minds may change. Besides, it were a mock

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Apt to be render’d, for someone to say

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: “Break up the senate till another time,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: When Caesar’s wife shall meet with better dreams.”

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: If Caesar hide himself, shall they not whisper

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: “Lo, Caesar is afraid”?

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Pardon me, Caesar; for my dear dear love

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: To our proceeding bids me tell you this;

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: And reason to my love is liable.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: How foolish do your fears seem now, Calpurnia!

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I am ashamed I did yield to them.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Give me my robe, for I will go.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And look where Publius is come to fetch me.

lines.speaker: PUBLIUS
lines.text: Good morrow, Caesar.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Welcome, Publius.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What, Brutus, are you stirr’d so early too?

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Good morrow, Casca. Caius Ligarius,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Caesar was ne’er so much your enemy

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: As that same ague which hath made you lean.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What is’t o’clock?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Caesar, ’tis strucken eight.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I thank you for your pains and courtesy.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: See! Antony, that revels long o’ nights,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Is notwithstanding up. Good morrow, Antony.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: So to most noble Caesar.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Bid them prepare within:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I am to blame to be thus waited for.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Now, Cinna: now, Metellus: what, Trebonius!

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I have an hour’s talk in store for you;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Remember that you call on me to-day:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Be near me, that I may remember you.

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: Caesar, I will: aside and so near will I be,

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: That your best friends shall wish I had been further.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Good friends, go in, and taste some wine with me;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And we, like friends, will straightway go together.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That every like is not the same, O Caesar,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The heart of Brutus yearns to think upon!

stage_directions.text: Thunder and lightning. Enter Caesar, in his night-gown.
Enter Publius, Brutus, Ligarius, Metellus, Casca, Trebonius, and Cinna.

glosses.phrase: Thrice hath Calpurnia in her sleep cried out
glosses.definition: three times in sleep Calpurnia has shouted — a significant number in Roman augury
immediate ritual offering — to read omens from the victim's entrails
insisted on ritual observances; been superstitious

glosses.phrase: lioness hath whelped in the streets
glosses.definition: a lioness has given birth in the streets — a Roman prodigy; such unnatural events portended disaster
graves have opened wide (yawned) and given up their dead — the standard apocalyptic prodigy

glosses.phrase: fiery warriors fought upon the clouds
glosses.definition: armies seen battling in the air — a portent recorded in Plutarch and classical historians for Caesar's death
crashed; clashed noisily
beyond all common experience; outside the natural order

glosses.phrase: When beggars die, there are no comets seen
glosses.definition: the heavens do not mark commoners' deaths with portents — only princes'. Calpurnia's political cosmology justifies her fear

glosses.phrase: Cowards die many times before their deaths
glosses.definition: the cowardly imagine their death often before it comes — Caesar's famous line justifying reckless valour
Roman priests (augurs) who read signs from bird-flight, animal entrails, and weather

glosses.phrase: Plucking the entrails of an offering
glosses.definition: tearing out the guts of a sacrificial animal — haruspicy, Roman divination from entrails

glosses.phrase: could not find a heart within the beast
glosses.definition: the sacrificial animal had no heart — the worst possible omen in Roman augury; the heart was the seat of life

glosses.phrase: two lions litter'd in one day
glosses.definition: twin lions born the same day — Caesar figures himself and Danger as twins, himself the elder
your wisdom is swallowed up by overconfidence
to humour you; for your mood
formal greeting — 'hail to you all'
old men (the senators)
statue (Elizabethan Latinate variant spelling)
vigorous; hearty
a lucky, favourable dream

glosses.phrase: from you great Rome shall suck / Reviving blood
glosses.definition: Rome will draw revitalising blood from Caesar — Decius's deliberate misreading, flattering Caesar as a saintly source of national life
colours, blood-marks, keepsakes and badges of office — the tokens believers took from a martyr's body; Decius turns the dream into sanctification
a mockery that is likely to be made
fever, especially a malarial one with alternating chills
stays up late partying

glosses.phrase: aside and so near will I be
glosses.definition: I will be aside but so near — Trebonius's ominous aside; he plans to detain Antony outside during the murder

glosses.phrase: That every like is not the same
glosses.definition: a friend in appearance is not always a friend in fact — Brutus's mournful aside

annotations.title: Calpurnia's plea: 2.1 inverted
annotations.body:
The scene is the exact structural mirror of Portia's in 2.1. A wife pleads with her husband to share his state of mind and to change his course. The husband initially resists. Here Calpurnia reports dream-prodigies and physical signs — the lioness whelping, graves yawning, warriors in the clouds — all taken from Plutarch and from stock Roman lists of assassination-year portents. She is, briefly, successful: Caesar yields ('Mark Antony shall say I am not well').

And then Decius arrives and reinterprets the dream. Shakespeare's stagecraft is merciless: Calpurnia's correct reading is displaced by Decius's flattering one, and Caesar dies within the hour. The scene enacts the reversal that 2.1 only set up. Portia wins her plea and is simply forgotten in the rush of plot; Calpurnia wins hers and is overridden by a skilled flatterer. Both wives are correct. Neither saves her husband.

Plutarch's *Life of Caesar* §63 records the dream, Caesar's initial yielding, and the manipulation that reverses it. Shakespeare adds the specific rhetoric — 'tinctures, stains, relics and cognizance' — which reads Calpurnia's nightmare as saintly martyrdom rather than murder.

annotations.title: 'Cowards die many times before their deaths' — Caesar's philosophy
annotations.body:
Caesar's famous couplet offers a Stoic-inflected theory of fortitude: the fearful rehearse death constantly, the brave face it only once. The lines are rhetorically magnificent and philosophically coherent. They are also the words of a man who, moments earlier, sent priests to sacrifice and read entrails; whose wife's three nightmares have unnerved him; who has been half-argued out of attending the Senate.

Shakespeare is not exposing Caesar as hypocrite — that would be too small. He is showing that the public philosophical self-image and the private superstitious reality coexist in the same man, and that the Caesar who will walk into the Senate in 3.1 is the Caesar holding both at once. The valiant-coward distinction is sincerely held; it is also, at the moment it is spoken, already a disavowal of the Caesar who was about to stay home.

annotations.title: Decius's re-reading: the dream turned inside out
annotations.body:
Calpurnia's dream of a statue running blood with Romans bathing their hands in it is clearly a premonition of the assassination. Decius reinterprets it as sanctification: Rome shall 'suck / Reviving blood' from Caesar, and men shall preserve 'tinctures, stains, relics and cognizance' — the vocabulary of relic-hunting at a martyr's shrine.

The reinterpretation works because Caesar wants it to. Decius tells Caesar what he most desires to hear: that the crowd will crown him today. The flattery is baited with just enough mockery ('it were a mock / Apt to be render'd' that Caesar's wife has bad dreams) to sting Caesar's honour. The three-step technique — flatter the ambition, shame the hesitation, rewrite the ominous sign — is as old as courtly politics and as current as contemporary demagoguery. Antony will use the same technique on the crowd in 3.2. Decius rehearses it here, in miniature, on Caesar.

trials.prompt: The Calpurnia scene (2.2) functions structurally as:
options: A mirror of Portia's 2.1 scene — the same wife-plea structure, the same dismissal, the same husband's death following shortly after

trials.prompt: 'Cowards die many times before their deaths' reveals:
options: A Stoic-ish public self-image held by a Caesar who has nonetheless already been rattled by his wife's dreams and sent priests to sacrifice — the public philosophy and private superstition coexist in him

trials.prompt: Decius successfully reinterprets Calpurnia's dream because:
options: He tells Caesar exactly what Caesar wants to hear — that the dream foretells sanctification and that the Senate will crown him today — and mixes in just enough shame at being cowed by a wife's fear
Calpurnia wavers on her own reading

trials.prompt: What is the worst omen the augurers deliver to Caesar?
options: A sacrificial animal with no heart — the worst possible haruspicial omen in Roman religion

trials.prompt: Trebonius's aside 'so near will I be, / That your best friends shall wish I had been further' functions as:
options: Ominous foreshadowing — Trebonius is planning to detain Antony outside during the murder
